speaker: interviewer
text: November 3, 1996. Survivor, Esther Clifford Ebe. Interviewer, Irene Dansky, in Cranbury, New Jersey, United States. The interview will be in English. Today is November 3, 1996. I'm Irene Dansky, and we're going to interview Esther Clifford in Cranbury, New Jersey, in the United States. And this interview is going to be in English. Can you tell us your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name, is Esther Clifford.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. E-S-T-H-E-R and C-L-I-F-F-O-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- my name was Ebe, E-B-E. My last name was Ebe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 5, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-N-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell your parents' name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father's name was Abraham Ebe. And my mother's name, Selda in Germany, because she was called Sophie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell her first name and her maiden name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Selda was spelled S-E-L-D-A, and her maiden name was Eiba, E-I-B-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born where?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Poland, in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also born in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you tell us the name, one at a time, of your sisters and brother, and spell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. My oldest sister's name is Regina, which is spelled R-E-G-I-N-A, Rosenthal, R-O-S-E-N-T-H-A-L. The next sister is Mary, M-A-R-Y, Halberstad, H-A-L-B-E-R-S-T-A-D. I had a middle sister whose name was Rosa, R-O-S-A, last name Ebe. My brother's name was Leo, L-E-O. And my name, I told you about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you raised in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was raised in Frankfurt. My parents went to Frankfurt to live there, to live in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they leave Poland to go to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they came to Germany before World War I started. It must have been between 1913 and 14. I don't know the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they left Poland to go to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were told that they went to Germany to seek a better life for their children. Germany was known as a country for good education. They had two children at the time, and they lived in Poland. And they wanted to provide a good education for the two children and the ones that were yet to come. And also, Poland was not-- there was-- there were always pogroms going on against the Jews. Pogroms. [LAUGHS] And they just wanted to seek a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they settle in? They didn't settle in Frankfurt right away. Did they settle in Munich when they first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, they settled in Munich, and they had to leave Munich. It had something to do, as far as I remember, with the Hitlerputsch that took place in the early-- I believe it was around 1920. And they always told us that they had settled in Munich, and-- but they had to leave Munich. And they-- then they went to various cities. They went to Hannover. They lived in Offenbach for awhile. And then they finally settled in Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born in 1920. And they went to Frankfurt in 19-- about '21. It was-- I wasn't a year old yet when they moved to Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family life like as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father owned a small leather goods store. And he made a fair living, not very-- not a great living, but a fair living. And I-- I always remember that being the youngest, I remember we were five-- we were five children, and it was always a little bit of a struggle. But I remember very good times. We were a happy family, always singing and dancing. And I especially remember the holidays. We would sit around the table and celebrate holiday-- holidays. And we would all talk about the future. My oldest sister, who married already in 1929, she would come with her married family to our house, and we-- and she would talk about her new husband. And then my-- the next sister, Mary, would-- she talked about-- she was very artistically inclined, and she would show us all the wonderful things that she had made. And my middle sister would talk about her future. She wanted to-- she was also artistically inclined, and she wanted to join my sister Mary with-- with whatever they were doing. They were going to go into some kind of business. My brother had a wonderful voice. He was a soloist at the Borneplatz Synagogue in Frankfurt. And he wanted to become a cantor. And I always wanted to be a movie star in Hollywood. I was thinking of going to Hollywood one day and become a movie star. At this time, I'm talking now about I'm now talking about 1938. So but all in all, we were very happy. And we had a record player, and we would play music. And-- and my parents were always busy working away, providing for their children. We were a very close family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a very small apartment. We lived right behind the store, behind that leather store that my father had. And I remember there was a kitchen, two bedrooms where all of us children slept, and it was a small house. But it was behind the leather store, on the ground floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else live with you besides your parents and siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we had a lot of company-- cousins from Munich would come and visit many times. We're always looking forward to that. And no-- but otherwise, not in our apartment, nobody else but our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Leopold?

speaker: interviewee
text: How about-- I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a cousin that stayed with you for awhile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, there was a cousin in Munich who came to Frankfurt quite frequently to attend the yeshiva. He wanted to become a cantor. It so happens that we all seemed to have good voices in our family. And he wanted to become a cantor. And he came to visit the yeshiva, and many times he stayed in our house. He lives now in California and is a cantor. Has been a cantor most of his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold Szneer. We called him Poldy. Szneer, right. And he's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poldy? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, his last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- Szneer, S-Z-N-E-E-R. He lives now in Beverly Hills and sings in a synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Frankfurt, we-- my parents attended what they used to call a [INAUDIBLE]. Sometimes it's called shtibel. It's a small synagogue where mostly people prayed that came from Poland. They spoke-- amongst themselves, they always spoke Yiddish. And we also went along with them to that synagogue. But as we got older, we would go to the more modern synagogues. Frankfurt had many modern synagogues. And there was especially one that was called the Borneplatz Synagogue, that I mentioned before already. So the-- the children, we, many times, went to-- most of the time, we went to these modern synagogues, mostly this Borneplatz Synagogue. There was also another synagogue called Breuer, B-R-E-- B-R-E-U-E-R. And that was a more orthodox synagogue. We were not-- we were orthodox, but not Hasidic orthodox, what we would call today Hasidic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we children kind of went back and forth. We would go to the big synagogue, go back to the [INAUDIBLE] that my parents used to called it, [INAUDIBLE]. Visited our parents and tell them where we are and then walk out again and go to the other synagogue. So that was our way of life on-- on Saturdays and holidays, Jewish holidays. Would you spell Borneplatz for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's B-O-R-N-E-P-L-A-T-Z synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what basically was the difference between the two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Borneplatz synagogue was visited mostly by Germans, not the ones who originated in Poland. They would speak German in that synagogue, while the [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] would-- people would speak Yiddish. As we grew up in Germany, we children would be more-- would-- liked better to be amongst people that were Germans, because that was where we were-- where we grew up. While my parents enjoyed rather being amongst people that they knew from Poland or that spoke their language. We understood Yiddish when they spoke to us. But we children, growing up in Germany, spoke German. We would call it Hochdeutsche in German. [LAUGHS] Hochdeutsche means well-spoken German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the synagogue that your parents attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the street. The name of the street was called Schwanestrasse. So the synagogue was called Schwanestrasse Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it-- it's spelled S-C-H-W-A-N-ES-T-R-A-S-S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did your father attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he attended quite often, every Friday night and Saturday, and also many times early in the morning. Every day, early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your favorite holidays as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course Hanukkah, [LAUGHS] I guess. But all the holidays, actually, were favorite. We liked holidays because as I mentioned before, we were a close family. We enjoyed holidays. We were all together. Usually my-- as I said, my oldest sister was married, and she would be with her married family and my-- and we all went our different ways to all kinds of schools. And-- but holidays, we were all together. And there was much talking going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any other family members? Extended family, grandparents, or aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we had-- sometimes we had cousins visiting us. But-- well, yes, I-- I remember, yes. We had an uncle, my-- my mother's brother, who lived in Hannover. He would come many times over holiday, and he would sing, and sing all of those Yiddish songs that he knew from Poland. And we were just so fascinated by him singing these songs. We would sing along. And we would laugh. We looked so forward to him coming, because he always came with new songs. And as I mentioned before, we just loved singing and loved hearing him sing. And these were-- and many times we had various people come to visit, yes. And sometimes we were amongst ourselves. But we-- I always remember a fairly full house. My sisters and brother would bring friends. And so as again, as I mentioned before, they were very happy times. Thinking back, we really lived very happy times, as I said before. We were not well to do, but we were very happy. Sometimes there was one bar of chocolate in the middle of the table, and everybody had a piece of chocolate, and that meant an awful lot. We were all happy with a small piece of chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything special about Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was a big holiday, yes. I remember my mother would make a certain wine from raisins, a raisin wine. She would call it [GERMAN] wine in Germany. And we all drank from that. And we enjoyed Passover, just like all the other holidays. But it was something special because as-- even today, it's very special, because we eat matzoh instead of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now we are talking about-- Hitler came on power in 1933. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started school in 1926. School was-- I used to go to a school called Philantropin, P-H-I-L-A-N-T-R-O-P-I-N. All of five-- all of us five children used to visit that school, Philantropin. Philantropin was a Jewish school. Not a yeshiva, a Jewish school that was very well-known all over Germany. It-- Jewish students came from everywhere. And it-- we visited-- all of us visited that school. And actually, we all liked going to school, my oldest sister especially. She was kind of a genius. She was very bright, and she jumped classes many times. And the teachers were very-- whenever they heard an Ebe would come to the Philantropin, they were thrilled because they thought we were all going to be as smart as my oldest sister. But none of us was as smart. [LAUGHS] She was very special. I liked going to school until Hitler came on power. Then it was hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- it was hard in  a way that-- when-- the minute Hitler came in power, it was hard-- everything got much harder. You would go-- even not talking about school, even if you go-- went to any public place, like the post office, or the-- anyplace, you would see these banners that said-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] 'The Jews are our misfortune.' We had never seen that before. And let me talk a little bit about the time before Hitler came on power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just the year-- in 1932-- I remember that my parents started getting quite worried because of his campaigning. He was campaigning. It was depression time, and he was campaigning and talking-- saying to the people that all we have to do to have a better life is to get rid of the Jews. That, of course-- and I remember myself, that was very depressing. But my parents were worried but not really that concerned, because they always used to say-- my father used to call him 'clown' in-- in German. He used to call him the-- the [GERMAN], which is a clown. And they were concerned, but not terribly worried, because nobody would think this-- the Germans, this-- the Germans, who had the best scientists, the best doctors, the greatest authors in the world, that they would allow to-- for Hitler to get on power. However, in 1933, we were-- it was like waking up one morning, and he was the leader. He became the leader like, overnight. And from then on, they got a little bit more concerned. Not terribly concerned, but a little bit more concerned. They just thought that somehow he would-- he would not last. In some way, he couldn't last. However, he lasted. And so after 1933, as-- as soon as he came on power, things had gotten bad. Because there was-- I used to have friends, Christian friends. We used to play, we never knew a difference. We knew a difference in so far that we were Jewish, they were Christians. But I remember very well that came-- came Christmas, they would invite me-- my friend would invite me to come to her house, and she would show me the Christmas tree. And came Hanukkah, she would come to our house, and we would show her the-- the Hanukkah lamp. And we would exchange little presents. And as soon as Hitler came on power, you could feel that-- I could feel that they didn't want to play with me anymore, and that was very hard for a child. I remember that I took that very serious. I couldn't even understand in the beginning why my friends avoided me until my parents told me, well, Hitler is on power, and they probably don't want-- their parents probably don't want their children to play with Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one after the other happened. My parents had friends, non-Jewish friends, in the building, and you could see that they avoided them. And-- and this is how it started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember, even back in 1933, the first thing that I really experienced, the-- what the Nazis were up to was, when I went with my mother shopping, and right there, it was getting a little dark, and right in the middle of a shopping area, they-- there was a bonfire, a fire right in the middle of the street. And people came and held books written by Jewish authors, and they were throwing them in that fire. And they had the children with them, and they handed them-- the books to the children. And the children threw the books into that bonfire in the middle of the street. And I remember getting-- I was depressed about that, because not so much the grownups threw the books [LAUGHS] in the fire, but the children. And I even recognized some of them from playing, from not so much school, because I went to a Jewish school, but you learned to-- you-- you-- you recognize people. You recognize children, especially. And that was the first thing that depressed me. And then of course these banners, when you walked into the public places that I mentioned before, was hard to take. And then, in time, people-- you heard about things-- people were intimidated, a cousin was-- was rolled into some water, and he had to get out of the water. He had a hard time, and he just about made it. That was already back in 1933. And then as-- as the years went by, like in 1934, there was a terrible paper being published. It was called the [NON-ENGLISH], Stormtrooper newspaper. Everything seemed to be stormtroopers. Troopers. And that was a horrible paper. That affected me terribly, because it was-- it-- it had all-- it-- it was plastered on all these stores that sold newspapers. And in the morning, going to school, I would see these [NON-ENGLISH], and people standing and reading them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they list in the [NON-ENGLISH]? They-- they had people-- faces with long noses and saliva running from their mouth and looking at-- of course, typically, you know, it meant Jewish people. 'Cuz there were-- they always talk-- they always wrote about Jewish people with their long noses. And they were looking at little Gentile children, greedily looking at the money that the children had in their hands, like they wanted to steal the money from these children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had terrible stories in there-- that Jewish doctors trying to inject Gentiles with poisonous medication. They had bankers that would swindle Jews out of their money. And here I was walking to school, and people were actually reading this and-- young people, old people, of all ages. And by the time I got to school, I couldn't learn. It was-- I-- I wasn't thinking. I wasn't listening to the teacher. I was thinking about that [NON-ENGLISH], what they had read in there. Because-- not that I stopped to read it, but somehow, once in a while, we had gotten a hold of it. And my brothers would bring it home, and we would read it just out of interest, what they had to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going to school was getting harder and harder. And-- and this-- and that was about '33. And then they had-- in 1934, I remember-- I'm talking about the highlights that come to my mind-- then they had the infamous Hitlerjugend, Hitler Youth.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a group that Hitler had somehow came up with, this Hitlerjugend. And they were given-- this was depression time, there wasn't much to-- to wear. And this Hitlerjugend, these young boys, were given beautiful uniforms, so that they would feel good, similar to what-- how a Boy Scout feels when he gets a new uniform. And these were young people. And they could actually do whatever they wanted with the children. They usually-- you know, they usually-- they didn't stop adult people, but children had a hard time, because you usually-- When you saw them come-- I remember when I walked to school, and I saw them come around the corner, I would turn around, or go on the other side, or try to avoid them. Many times I came to school very late, just trying to avoid these Hitlerjugend. In time, these children would get to know the Jewish children, and they would wait for them. And many times I had to pass another school, a non-Jewish school, and they would stand there waiting for me. They would th-- throw stones, not just at me, at many of the Jewish children. So I would have to find all kinds of ways to get to school in order to avoid either these Delinquent or the-- the-- the the other children in school. And I remember, I think it was 1935. When my brother used to take violin lessons, and one day, he wasn't home on time. When somebody wasn't home on time, we always worried, because we always wondered. But nobody wanted to show the other person. We didn't want our parents to worry. Nobody wanted anybody else to worry. And I remember that I could-- I remember everybody looking at the clock, nobody saying much. And then I started-- I put my coat, and I went out, and I thought, let me see what happened to him. Let me look for him. And he was on the floor, with his violin broken in pieces. And the Hitlerjugend were beating him up. [SIGHS] It was terrible. And-- Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made it home, and he had to recover from the-- from this incident. It was very hard to take. I also, that particular year, I remember being in a-- in a bus coming home. At that time, I took the-- the-- the-- it wasn't a bus, it was a trolley, what today like a trolley car. And they were also pushing me around in that bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I got off the bus at the next stop, I remember thinking, I didn't-- I could under-- not understand, but these were children, or young people-- they weren't such children anymore. But there were grown up, adult people on that bus. And I always wondered, how can--

speaker: interviewer
text: It bothered me more than being pushed around by children. Why didn't the adult people tell them anything? Why didn't they do something about it? Why didn't they come to my help? So that is what I remember in those years.
